title: The Rent Roll Multiple: What Actually Determines What a Real Estate Agency Is Worth
linkedin:
Every real estate agency on the East Coast just finished its annual rent reviews. Letters went out, tenants absorbed the increase, and the property management team moved straight on to the next settlement without much ceremony.

That quiet week is also the one that says the most about what the agency is actually worth.

Most owners track one number: total revenue, sales commission and property management fees folded into a single trading result. A buyer sees something different. They see two businesses under one name, and only one behaves like an asset.

Sales commission is exciting and unreliable in equal measure, driven by interest rates and listing volumes that have nothing to do with how well the agency is run. Property management fee income renews monthly whether the market is buoyant or flat.

Rent rolls sell constantly in Australia, always on a multiple of annual management fee income. That multiple moves on landlord retention, average tenure, and how much the book depends on the owner personally rather than on a team and a system.

Ask most owners for their retention rate and you get a confident guess, not a number pulled from the system. It is one of the first things a buyer insists on seeing precisely, because it predicts the future far better than this month's fee total does.

None of this requires the agency to be for sale. A rent roll that retains well is simply a more valuable business to run, long before anyone asks to see the number.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #BrisbaneSME #SMEFinance

Here's the thing though: that's the week that actually shows what the agency is worth. Sales commission feels like the exciting part, but it swings with interest rates and listing volumes. Property management fee income just quietly renews every month, market or no market.

Rent rolls sell on a multiple of that fee income, and the multiple depends heavily on one number almost nobody tracks precisely: landlord retention.

Quick question for agency owners: do you actually know your retention rate, or is it a confident guess?

google:
A note for real estate agency owners on the East Coast as the July rent review round wraps up. It's easy to treat this as routine admin and move straight on to the next settlement, but this is also the week that says the most about what your agency is actually worth.

Most owners watch one combined number: sales commission and property management fees together on the P&L. A buyer, or anyone assessing the business properly, sees two very different revenue streams. Sales commission swings with the market. Property management fee income renews monthly regardless of conditions, and it's the one that behaves like a real asset.

Rent rolls in Australia are bought and sold on a multiple of annual management fee income, and that multiple depends on landlord retention, average tenure, and how dependent the book is on the owner personally. Most agencies can state their fee total instantly but have never pinned down their actual retention rate, even though it's one of the first things that gets asked for in a proper valuation.

If you run a property management business on the Queensland, New South Wales or Victorian coast, this is worth ten minutes of thought before the next rent review cycle comes around. Read the full piece on the blog for what actually moves the multiple.
